title: Choosing for Cloud vs. Edge
concept: Backbone choice depends on deployment environment: cloud systems can often afford larger models and centralized hardware, while edge systems must respect device limits, connectivity, privacy, and real-time response on local hardware.

exercises.prompt: Recap: why might a slightly less accurate backbone still be the better product choice?

options.text: Because lower latency can create a better real user experience
Because backbones do not run on hardware

exercises.explain: Right — and the hardware environment decides how important that speed difference feels.

cards.title: The same backbone lives differently in a rack and in a pocket
cards.body: A model that feels comfortable on a cloud GPU may feel clumsy on a phone or small camera. Deployment changes everything: available compute, battery, heat, network delay, privacy constraints, and whether the model must keep working when offline.

cards.title: Cloud and edge create different backbone pressures
cards.body: In the cloud, images are sent to centralized hardware for inference. On the edge, inference happens on the device itself. Cloud setups can often handle larger backbones more easily. Edge setups usually reward efficient backbones that fit local compute, memory, and energy limits.

cards.title: Warehouse versus backpack
cards.body: A warehouse gives you room for big tools and spare parts. A backpack forces careful packing. Cloud deployment is more like the warehouse; edge deployment is more like the backpack. The job may be the same, but the carrying capacity is not.

cards.title: Bad signal can change the right answer
cards.body: Suppose a phone app must identify subway signs underground. Even if a cloud model is strong, sending every frame over a weak network may feel slow or fail entirely. A lighter on-device backbone can win because it works privately, locally, and reliably without depending on connection quality.

cards.title: Edge does not mean “tiny at all costs”
cards.body: Edge deployment often favors lighter backbones, but the exact limit depends on the device. A modern phone with an accelerator can handle more than a tiny sensor node. The lesson is not “always choose the smallest model.” The lesson is “choose for the real hardware and operating conditions.”

cards.title: Two deployment worlds
cards.body: Backbone choice changes when the runtime environment changes.

left.text:
Centralized compute
Can often tolerate heavier models
Network round-trip becomes part of latency

right.text:
Runs on local hardware
Battery, memory, and heat matter
Can work offline and preserve privacy

exercises.prompt: Which situation most naturally fits a cloud-first backbone choice?

options.text: A central service processing uploaded catalog photos on server hardware
A battery-powered trail camera with unreliable connectivity
A phone app that must keep working underground without signal
A wearable device with very tight local power limits

exercises.explain: Correct — centralized server processing is the classic cloud-friendly setting.

exercises.prompt: True or false: deployment environment can matter as much as raw model accuracy when choosing a backbone.
exercises.explain: True — where the model runs changes what “good” means.

exercises.sentence: On-device inference is especially attractive when privacy, low latency, or ___ operation matters.
exercises.explain: Exactly — being able to keep working without the network is a major edge advantage.

exercises.prompt: Which deployment scenario most strongly pushes you toward an efficient edge backbone?

options.text: A pill-identification app that must run privately on a phone even with no connection
A server rack dedicated to bulk image analysis
A slideshow playing in an empty room

exercises.explain: Yes — privacy plus unreliable connectivity strongly favors on-device inference.

exercises.prompt: A company wants a tunnel-inspection vision tool. Internet access is weak inside tunnels, and workers do not want to upload every frame. What backbone direction is most sensible?

options.text: Choose an efficient backbone that can run on the local device, because offline use and privacy matter
Choose the heaviest cloud-only backbone and assume the network will always cooperate
Ignore deployment environment, because only benchmark accuracy matters
Skip feature extraction entirely and rely on luck

exercises.explain: Exactly. Cloud versus edge is not just infrastructure trivia — it directly changes the right backbone choice. Next: even after hardware is clear, you still choose differently for classification, detection, segmentation, and other tasks.
